👶 Bronchiolitis in Babies

A common viral chest infection in babies and young infants.
Dr. Murali Gopal
Senior Paediatrician & Paediatric Pulmonologist
MCR: 57489
MBBS, DCH(UK), MRCPCH(UK), FRCPCH(UK), CCT Paediatrics (UK), Fellow in Paediatric Pulmonology (Aus), Allergology (Ind)
Last reviewed: 10 May 2026
Reassurance for parentsBronchiolitis is very common in babies, especially in the first year of life. It usually gets better with time and supportive care, but some babies become breathless or feed poorly and need urgent review.

What is Bronchiolitis?

Bronchiolitis is a viral chest infection that affects the tiny airways in the lungs, called bronchioles. Swelling and mucus make breathing and feeding more difficult.

Symptoms & Signs

  • Runny nose at the start of the illness
  • Cough or wheeze
  • Fast breathing or increased effort of breathing
  • Poor feeding or tiring while feeding
  • Fever may occur

Causes

  • Most often caused by respiratory viruses such as RSV
  • Most common in babies under 12 months
  • Spreads through droplets, hands, and close contact with infected secretions

Home Management

  • Offer smaller, more frequent feeds.
  • Use saline nose drops and gentle nasal suction before feeds if advised.
  • Avoid cigarette smoke exposure.
  • Monitor wet nappies and alertness.
  • Do not give over-the-counter cough and cold medicines to infants unless specifically advised.

Red Flags / When to Seek Help

  • Breathing becomes very fast or difficult
  • Pauses in breathing, grunting, or chest sucking in
  • Poor feeding, fewer wet nappies, or signs of dehydration
  • Blue lips or skin
  • Baby is very sleepy, floppy, or hard to wake

Important Facts / Myth correction

Bronchiolitis is usually viral, so antibiotics do not routinely help.
There is no routine medicine that makes bronchiolitis go away faster.
The cough may last longer than the worst part of the illness.

Legal medical disclaimer

This parent guide is for education only and does not replace a medical consultation, diagnosis, or treatment plan. Advice should be interpreted in the context of your child's age, symptoms, and medical history. If your child has worsening symptoms, breathing difficulty, dehydration, unusual drowsiness, persistent high fever, seizures, or any emergency warning signs, seek urgent medical care immediately. All final guides should be reviewed by a qualified medical professional before clinical use.

References

  • Royal Children’s Hospital Melbourne. Kids Health Info: Bronchiolitis. https://www.rch.org.au/kidsinfo/fact_sheets/bronchiolitis/
